content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:gpt4_ec93e27f

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The forced memory source contains durable personal travel facts, dated events, current/future trip plans, and a reusable sustainability/travel-pattern summary.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        - profile benchmark:longmemeval:gpt4_ec93e27f:profile:<0> Profile for User (USER).
Stable facts:
- The user was trying to reduce their carbon footprint.
- The user was planning a trip to Japan in May, including Tokyo and Osaka.
- The user wanted eco-friendly accommodation recommendations in Tokyo and Osaka for the Japan trip.
- The user was planning to take the train from Tokyo city center to Narita Airport for the Japan trip.
- The user had travel-planning experience for Japan by discussing accommodations, Narita Airport transit, train sustainability, scenic train routes, and station baggage services for a planned Japan trip.
- The user learned about Japan's Airport Check-in and Baggage Drop-off or Station Check-in service for checking in and dropping luggage at train stations before going to Narita Airport.
- note hot-path-4ab6852410db-note-2: CONTEXT; Japan trip planning emphasizes low-carbon travel; For the user's May Japan trip planning, recommendations were framed around reducing carbon footprint by choosing eco-friendly accommodations, rail travel, and scenic train routes, with buses noted as potentially eco-friendly for shorter distances but trains generally better for longer distances.; scope=User's Japan trip planning context involving Tokyo, Osaka, Narita Airport, and eco-friendly travel choices
- claim hot-path-4ab6852410db-claim-7: has_goal; The user was planning a trip to Japan in May, including Tokyo and Osaka.; scope=User travel planning context from the 2023-02-27 imported chat session.
- claim hot-path-4ab6852410db-claim-8: has_goal; The user wanted eco-friendly accommodation recommendations in Tokyo and Osaka for the Japan trip.; scope=User preferences and constraints for the Japan trip discussed in the 2023-02-27 imported chat session.
- claim hot-path-4ab6852410db-claim-12: has_goal; The user was planning to take the train from Tokyo city center to Narita Airport for the Japan trip.; scope=User transit plan for the Japan trip discussed in the 2023-02-27 imported chat session.
- claim hot-path-4ab6852410db-claim-13: has_experience_with; The user learned about Japan's Airport Check-in and Baggage Drop-off or Station Check-in service for checking in and dropping luggage at train stations before going to Narita Airport.; scope=Reusable travel advice from the imported 2023-02-27 Japan trip-planning conversation.
- claim hot-path-4ab6852410db-claim-10: has_goal; The user was trying to reduce their carbon footprint.; scope=User sustainability goal stated in the 2023-02-27 imported chat session.
- claim hot-path-4ab6852410db-claim-11: has_experience_with; The user had travel-planning experience for Japan by discussing accommodations, Narita Airport transit, train sustainability, scenic train routes, and station baggage services for a planned Japan trip.; scope=Travel-planning experience evidenced by the 2023-02-27 imported chat session.
- note hot-path-4ab6852410db-note-6: PLAN; Station check-in before Narita Airport train transfer; The transcript described a possible "Airport Check-in and Baggage Drop-off" or "Station Check-in" workflow before taking the train to Narita Airport: confirm airline participation, use a participating Tokyo station such as Tokyo Station, Shinjuku Station, Shibuya Station, or Ikebukuro Station, check in and print the boarding pass, drop luggage, then continue by train to the airport.; scope=Potential station check-in and baggage drop-off workflow for the user's Narita Airport departure
- note hot-path-4ab6852410db-note-3: CONTEXT; Narita Airport transit options from Tokyo city center; For travel from Tokyo city center to Narita Airport, the discussed options were Narita Express as the fastest train, Keisei Electric Railway as a cheaper rail option with Skyliner and Access Express variants, and buses as the cheapest but traffic-sensitive option; Suica/PASMO and Japan Rail Pass were noted as useful depending on route and eligibility.; scope=Narita Airport transfer planning from central Tokyo for the user's Japan trip
- note hot-path-4ab6852410db-note-4: CONTEXT; Japan rail sustainability context for route recommendations; Japan rail was presented as a relatively low-carbon transport mode, especially for longer distances, with JR East, JR Central, Odakyu Railway, and Keisei Electric Railway named as operators pursuing energy-efficient trains, renewable energy, or sustainability practices; N700 series Shinkansen, JR Kyushu's "Solar Train", hydrogen fuel cell trains, and carbon offsetting were cited as examples of eco-focused initiatives.; scope=Sustainability context for recommending rail travel in Japan
- note hot-path-4ab6852410db-note-5: CONTEXT; Scenic low-carbon rail ideas for Japan itinerary; The Japan itinerary discussion identified scenic train routes that align with the user's lower-carbon travel interest, including Tokaido Shinkansen for Mount Fuji views, Nikko Train, Kurobe Gorge Railway, Hakone Tozan Railway, Shinano Railway, Akkeshi Railway, Gono Line, Takayama Main Line, Kishin Line, and Izu Hakone Railway.; scope=Scenic train route options for the user's Japan itinerary
- claim hot-path-4ab6852410db-claim-9: attended_event; The user attended a friend's wedding on 2023-02-27 and had just returned from it by bus.; scope=Dated personal event from the imported 2023-02-27 chat session.

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I / my; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<0>; type=existing; name=I / my; reason=First-person statements refer to the stable namespace-level User entity.
- mention=my family in the countryside / my family's place; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=PERSON; name=User's family in the countryside; reason=A recurring concrete personal referent that later claims may target; no existing candidate matches.
- mention=the mountains / the mountains in the north; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=LOCATION; name=Mountains in the north; reason=Specific trip destination described in the target message; no existing candidate matches, though exact location is unspecified.
- mention=the city where I live / my starting point; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=LOCATION; name=User's city of residence; reason=Reusable location referent for the user's road trip origin; no existing candidate matches.
- mention=Japan; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<4>; type=existing; name=Japan; reason=Exact safe match to existing location entity.
- mention=Tokyo; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<5>; type=existing; name=Tokyo; reason=Exact safe match to existing location entity.
- mention=Osaka; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<6>; type=existing; name=Osaka; reason=Exact safe match to existing location entity.
- mention=Singapore; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=LOCATION; name=Singapore; reason=Concrete location mentioned as a repeated work travel destination; no existing candidate matches.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|family in the countryside|mountains in the north|Japan|Tokyo|Osaka|Singapore
        text_queries=trip to mountains April 15 detour family countryside route|train ride family countryside March 3 2-hour journey|reduced carbon emissions 20% past quarter trains buses instead of driving taxis|Japan trip in May 7th international trip this year Tokyo Osaka booked flights accommodation|averaging 2 flights per month since November last year work-related Singapore 3 times past 4 months January 20
        predicate_hints=has_goal|attended_event|metric_observation|current_metric_value|prefers|avoids

Source metadata: origin=provided_text; input_kind=TEXT; source_ref=longmemeval:gpt4_ec93e27f:session:2; title=LongMemEval gpt4_ec93e27f session 2; user_consent_confirmed=true; force_memory_write=true; mode=force
LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/03/03 (Fri) 14:37
Transcript:
user: I'm planning a trip to the mountains on April 15th and I'm considering taking a detour to visit my family in the countryside again. Can you help me find the best route for the road trip? By the way, I took a train ride to visit them today, and it was a nice 2-hour journey.
assistant: I'd be happy to help you plan your road trip!

However, I have to clarify that I'm a large language model, I don't have access to your personal information or locations. You didn't mention the specific mountains you're planning to visit, nor the location of your family in the countryside.

Could you please provide more details about your trip? Here are some questions to help me better assist you:

1. Where are the mountains you're planning to visit?
2. Where is your family's location in the countryside?
3. What is your starting point for the road trip?
4. Do you have a preferred route or mode of transportation in mind (e.g., highway, scenic route, tolls, etc.)?
5. How much time do you have available for the detour to visit your family?

Once I have this information, I can help you find the best route for your road trip!
user: I'm planning to visit the mountains in the north, and my family's place is actually on the way, so it's quite convenient. My starting point will be from the city where I live, and I'm flexible with the route, as long as it's not too far out of the way.
assistant: That sounds like a great plan!

Since you didn't mention specific cities or locations, I'll provide some general guidance to help you plan your road trip.

1. **Use a mapping or GPS tool**: Websites like Google Maps, Waze, or MapQuest can help you find the most efficient route from your city to the mountains, with a detour to your family's place. You can enter your starting point, destination, and intermediate stops to get turn-by-turn directions.
2. **Check road conditions**: Before you embark on your journey, check the road conditions, weather forecasts, and any road closures or construction along your planned route. This will help you prepare for any potential delays or route changes.
3. **Estimate travel time and distance**: Calculate the driving distance and estimated time between each leg of your journey. This will help you plan your itinerary, including how much time to spend with your family and when to depart for the mountains.
4. **Consider scenic routes or alternative roads**: If you have time and prefer a more leisurely drive, you might want to explore scenic routes or alternative roads that can offer more picturesque views or avoid heavy traffic.
5. **Plan for rest stops and breaks**: Don't forget to factor in rest stops, meal breaks, and fuel stops along the way. This will help you stay energized and focused during the drive.

Since you've taken a train ride to visit your family recently, you might have a rough idea of the distance and travel time. You can use this as a starting point to estimate the driving time and plan your road trip accordingly.

If you're willing to share more specific details about your locations, I'd be happy to provide more tailored advice or recommendations for your route.
user: I have been tracking my travel expenses and modes of transport, and I've noticed that I've reduced my carbon emissions by around 20% in the past quarter. I've been trying to reduce my carbon footprint, so I've been taking more trains and buses instead of driving or taking taxis.
assistant: That's a great achievement! Reducing your carbon emissions by 20% in just a quarter is a significant step towards a more sustainable lifestyle. It's fantastic that you're making conscious choices to minimize your impact on the environment.

Using public transportation, like trains and buses, is an excellent way to reduce carbon emissions. Not only do you reduce the number of cars on the road, but you also benefit from the economies of scale of shared transportation. According to the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), public transportation can reduce carbon emissions by 20 pounds per passenger per trip, compared to driving alone.

It's also great that you're tracking your travel expenses and modes of transport. Being aware of your habits and their impact is crucial to making informed decisions. By monitoring your progress, you can identify areas for further improvement and adjust your behavior accordingly.

Now, considering your upcoming road trip, you might want to explore options to minimize your carbon footprint during the journey. Here are a few suggestions:

1. **Carpool**: If possible, consider carpooling with friends or family members who are also traveling to the mountains. This can reduce the number of cars on the road and lower emissions.
2. **Fuel-efficient vehicle**: If you have access to a fuel-efficient vehicle, consider using it for the trip. Electric or hybrid vehicles are great options, but even a fuel-efficient gasoline-powered car can make a difference.
3. **Optimize your route**: As I mentioned earlier, use mapping tools to find the most efficient route to your destination. This can help reduce fuel consumption and lower emissions.
4. **Drive smoothly**: Adopt eco-friendly driving habits, such as maintaining a consistent speed, avoiding rapid acceleration, and using cruise control on the highway.
5. **Offset your emissions**: If you're unable to reduce your emissions further, consider offsetting them by investing in carbon offset projects or renewable energy initiatives.

Remember, every small change you make can add up to make a significant difference. Keep up the good work, and enjoy your road trip to the mountains!
user: I'm actually planning a trip to Japan in May, which will be my 7th international trip this year. I've already booked my flights and accommodation, and I'm excited to explore Tokyo and Osaka.
assistant: Wow, 7 international trips in a year is impressive! You must be a seasoned traveler!

Japan is an amazing country, and you've chosen two fantastic destinations in Tokyo and Osaka. Both cities offer a unique blend of traditional and modern culture, delicious food, and exciting experiences.

Since you've already booked your flights and accommodation, I assume you've got the logistics covered. Here are some tips to help you make the most of your trip:

1. **Get familiar with Japanese culture**: Japan has a unique culture that can be quite different from what you're used to. Researching customs, etiquette, and basic Japanese phrases will help you navigate the country more smoothly.
2. **Plan your itinerary**: With so much to see and do in Tokyo and Osaka, prioritize your must-visit attractions and experiences. Consider purchasing a Suica or Pasmo card for easy travel on public transportation.
3. **Foodie adventures**: Japan is a food lover's paradise! Be sure to try popular dishes like sushi, ramen, udon, and okonomiyaki. Don't be afraid to explore local markets and try some street food.
4. **Explore beyond the cities**: While Tokyo and Osaka are amazing, consider taking day trips or short excursions to nearby destinations like Nikko, Kamakura, or Nara to experience more of Japan's natural beauty and cultural heritage.
5. **Learn some Japanese phrases**: While many Japanese people speak some English, learning basic phrases like "konnichiwa" (hello), "arigatou" (thank you), and "sumimasen" (excuse me) will go a long way in showing respect for the culture and people.

Since you've taken multiple international trips this year, I'm curious:

* What's been your favorite destination so far?
* What are you most looking forward to experiencing in Japan?

Feel free to share your travel stories and tips!
user: I've been averaging around 2 flights per month since November last year, with most of them being work-related. In fact, I've been to Singapore 3 times in the past 4 months, with the last trip being on January 20th.
assistant: Wow, that's a lot of flying! Averaging 2 flights per month is quite frequent, especially with most of them being work-related. It's great that you're able to travel for work, but it can also be tiring and take a toll on your personal life.

It's interesting that you've been to Singapore three times in the past four months. Singapore is a great hub for business travel, and it's a wonderful city-state with a lot to offer. Did you have a chance to explore the city beyond your work commitments, or was it mostly focused on meetings and conferences?

Since you've been traveling so frequently, I'm curious:

* How do you manage your work-life balance with such a demanding travel schedule?
* What are some tips you can share for staying energized and focused during long flights and busy trips?
* Are there any specific airport lounges or travel perks that you particularly enjoy?

Feel free to share your experiences and advice!
user: I've actually been taking a lot of trains lately, especially for shorter distances. In fact, I took a train ride to visit my family in the countryside just recently, on March 3rd, and it was a nice 2-hour journey.
assistant: I think there's been a misunderstanding! Earlier, you mentioned taking flights, but now you're talking about taking trains. I'm happy to chat with you about your train travel experiences, but I want to clarify that we were previously discussing your frequent flights.

If you'd like to talk about your train travel, I'd be happy to hear about it! Taking trains can be a great way to travel, especially for shorter distances. It's often more environmentally friendly, and you can enjoy the scenery outside your window.

Could you tell me more about your recent train ride to visit your family in the countryside? What was the scenery like during the 2-hour journey? Was it a relaxing trip?

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>

        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:gpt4_ec93e27f

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The forced memory source contains durable personal travel facts, dated events, current/future trip plans, and a reusable sustainability/travel-pattern summary.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Session date: 2023/03/03 (Fri) 14:37
Transcript:
user: I'm planning a trip to the mountains on April 15th and I'm considering taking a detour to visit my family in the countryside again. Can you help me find the best route for the road trip? By the way, I took a train ride to visit them today, and it was a nice 2-hour journey.
assistant: I'd be happy to help you plan your road trip!

However, I have to clarify that I'm a large language model, I don't have access to your personal information or locations. You didn't mention the specific mountains you're planning to visit, nor the location of your family in the countryside.

Could you please provide more details about your trip? Here are some questions to help me better assist you:

1. Where are the mountains you're planning to visit?
2. Where is your family's location in the countryside?
3. What is your starting point for the road trip?
4. Do you have a preferred route or mode of transportation in mind (e.g., highway, scenic route, tolls, etc.)?
5. How much time do you have available for the detour to visit your family?

Once I have this information, I can help you find the best route for your road trip!
user: I'm planning to visit the mountains in the north, and my family's place is actually on the way, so it's quite convenient. My starting point will be from the city where I live, and I'm flexible with the route, as long as it's not too far out of the way.
assistant: That sounds like a great plan!

Since you didn't mention specific cities or locations, I'll provide some general guidance to help you plan your road trip.

1. **Use a mapping or GPS tool**: Websites like Google Maps, Waze, or MapQuest can help you find the most efficient route from your city to the mountains, with a detour to your family's place. You can enter your starting point, destination, and intermediate stops to get turn-by-turn directions.
2. **Check road conditions**: Before you embark on your journey, check the road conditions, weather forecasts, and any road closures or construction along your planned route. This will help you prepare for any potential delays or route changes.
3. **Estimate travel time and distance**: Calculate the driving distance and estimated time between each leg of your journey. This will help you plan your itinerary, including how much time to spend with your family and when to depart for the mountains.
4. **Consider scenic routes or alternative roads**: If you have time and prefer a more leisurely drive, you might want to explore scenic routes or alternative roads that can offer more picturesque views or avoid heavy traffic.
5. **Plan for rest stops and breaks**: Don't forget to factor in rest stops, meal breaks, and fuel stops along the way. This will help you stay energized and focused during the drive.

Since you've taken a train ride to visit your family recently, you might have a rough idea of the distance and travel time. You can use this as a starting point to estimate the driving time and plan your road trip accordingly.

If you're willing to share more specific details about your locations, I'd be happy to provide more tailored advice or recommendations for your route.
user: I have been tracking my travel expenses and modes of transport, and I've noticed that I've reduced my carbon emissions by around 20% in the past quarter. I've been trying to reduce my carbon footprint, so I've been taking more trains and buses instead of driving or taking taxis.
assistant: That's a great achievement! Reducing your carbon emissions by 20% in just a quarter is a significant step towards a more sustainable lifestyle. It's fantastic that you're making conscious choices to minimize your impact on the environment.

Using public transportation, like trains and buses, is an excellent way to reduce carbon emissions. Not only do you reduce the number of cars on the road, but you also benefit from the economies of scale of shared transportation. According to the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), public transportation can reduce carbon emissions by 20 pounds per passenger per trip, compared to driving alone.

It's also great that you're tracking your travel expenses and modes of transport. Being aware of your habits and their impact is crucial to making informed decisions. By monitoring your progress, you can identify areas for further improvement and adjust your behavior accordingly.

Now, considering your upcoming road trip, you might want to explore options to minimize your carbon footprint during the journey. Here are a few suggestions:

1. **Carpool**: If possible, consider carpooling with friends or family members who are also traveling to the mountains. This can reduce the number of cars on the road and lower emissions.
2. **Fuel-efficient vehicle**: If you have access to a fuel-efficient vehicle, consider using it for the trip. Electric or hybrid vehicles are great options, but even a fuel-efficient gasoline-powered car can make a difference.
3. **Optimize your route**: As I mentioned earlier, use mapping tools to find the most efficient route to your destination. This can help reduce fuel consumption and lower emissions.
4. **Drive smoothly**: Adopt eco-friendly driving habits, such as maintaining a consistent speed, avoiding rapid acceleration, and using cruise control on the highway.
5. **Offset your emissions**: If you're unable to reduce your emissions further, consider offsetting them by investing in carbon offset projects or renewable energy initiatives.

Remember, every small change you make can add up to make a significant difference. Keep up the good work, and enjoy your road trip to the mountains!
user: I'm actually planning a trip to Japan in May, which will be my 7th international trip this year. I've already booked my flights and accommodation, and I'm excited to explore Tokyo and Osaka.
assistant: Wow, 7 international trips in a year is impressive! You must be a seasoned traveler!

Japan is an amazing country, and you've chosen two fantastic destinations in Tokyo and Osaka. Both cities offer a unique blend of traditional and modern culture, delicious food, and exciting experiences.

Since you've already booked your flights and accommodation, I assume you've got the logistics covered. Here are some tips to help you make the most of your trip:

1. **Get familiar with Japanese culture**: Japan has a unique culture that can be quite different from what you're used to. Researching customs, etiquette, and basic Japanese phrases will help you navigate the country more smoothly.
2. **Plan your itinerary**: With so much to see and do in Tokyo and Osaka, prioritize your must-visit attractions and experiences. Consider purchasing a Suica or Pasmo card for easy travel on public transportation.
3. **Foodie adventures**: Japan is a food lover's paradise! Be sure to try popular dishes like sushi, ramen, udon, and okonomiyaki. Don't be afraid to explore local markets and try some street food.
4. **Explore beyond the cities**: While Tokyo and Osaka are amazing, consider taking day trips or short excursions to nearby destinations like Nikko, Kamakura, or Nara to experience more of Japan's natural beauty and cultural heritage.
5. **Learn some Japanese phrases**: While many Japanese people speak some English, learning basic phrases like "konnichiwa" (hello), "arigatou" (thank you), and "sumimasen" (excuse me) will go a long way in showing respect for the culture and people.

Since you've taken multiple international trips this year, I'm curious:

* What's been your favorite destination so far?
* What are you most looking forward to experiencing in Japan?

Feel free to share your travel stories and tips!
user: I've been averaging around 2 flights per month since November last year, with most of them being work-related. In fact, I've been to Singapore 3 times in the past 4 months, with the last trip being on January 20th.
assistant: Wow, that's a lot of flying! Averaging 2 flights per month is quite frequent, especially with most of them being work-related. It's great that you're able to travel for work, but it can also be tiring and take a toll on your personal life.

It's interesting that you've been to Singapore three times in the past four months. Singapore is a great hub for business travel, and it's a wonderful city-state with a lot to offer. Did you have a chance to explore the city beyond your work commitments, or was it mostly focused on meetings and conferences?

Since you've been traveling so frequently, I'm curious:

* How do you manage your work-life balance with such a demanding travel schedule?
* What are some tips you can share for staying energized and focused during long flights and busy trips?
* Are there any specific airport lounges or travel perks that you particularly enjoy?

Feel free to share your experiences and advice!
user: I've actually been taking a lot of trains lately, especially for shorter distances. In fact, I took a train ride to visit my family in the countryside just recently, on March 3rd, and it was a nice 2-hour journey.
assistant: I think there's been a misunderstanding! Earlier, you mentioned taking flights, but now you're talking about taking trains. I'm happy to chat with you about your train travel experiences, but I want to clarify that we were previously discussing your frequent flights.

If you'd like to talk about your train travel, I'd be happy to hear about it! Taking trains can be a great way to travel, especially for shorter distances. It's often more environmentally friendly, and you can enjoy the scenery outside your window.

Could you tell me more about your recent train ride to visit your family in the countryside? What was the scenery like during the 2-hour journey? Was it a relaxing trip?

responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor
responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.

structured.fullText: {"notes":[{"candidate_claim_hints":["The user tracks travel expenses and modes of transport.","The user reduced carbon emissions by around 20% in the past quarter.","The user has been taking more trains and buses instead of driving or taking taxis."],"confidence":0.96,"entity_refs":[{"entity_id":"entity:<0>","role":"primary"}],"evidence_kind":"direct","evidence_quote":"I've reduced my carbon emissions by around 20% in the past quarter. I've been trying to reduce my carbon footprint, so I've been taking more trains and buses instead of driving or taking taxis.","evidence_reason":"The quote directly states both the measured emissions reduction and the behavior changes behind it.","importance":4,"keywords":["carbon footprint","travel emissions","trains","buses","driving","taxis","sustainable travel"],"note_type":"context","rationale":"This is more than a single atomic fact: it captures a reusable behavioral pattern and planning lens for future travel recommendations.","scope_json":{"basis":"summarized","kind":"entity","text":"User travel-planning context across local trips, work travel, and international travel"},"summary":"The user tracks travel expenses and modes of transport and is actively shifting toward lower-carbon travel, reporting about a 20% carbon-emissions reduction in the past quarter by taking more trains and buses instead of driving or taxis. My starting point will be from the city where I live, and I'm flexible with the route, as long as it's not too far out of the way.","evidence_reason":"The quote directly describes the route constraints: family stop is on the way, origin is the user's city, and route flexibility depends on detour distance.","importance":3,"keywords":["April 15","mountains in the north","family countryside","road trip","detour","route planning"],"note_type":"plan","rationale":"This combines destination, timing, route constraints, and detour rationale into a reusable trip-planning context rather than only isolated claims.","scope_json":{"basis":"summarized","kind":"entity","text":"User's planned April 15 road trip from their city of residence to the mountains in the north via family in the countryside"},"summary":"For an April 15 trip to the mountains in the north, the user's family in the countryside is on the way from the user's city of residence, making a detour convenient as long as it is not too far out of the way. The user is flexible about the route, so future route planning should optimize for efficiency, reasonable detour distance, and possibly low-emission choices.","tags":["travel_plan","road_trip","family_visit"],"title":"April 15 mountain road trip can include family detour","valid_from":"2023-03-03T14:37:00+02:00","valid_to":"2023-04-15T23:59:59+03:00"},{"candidate_claim_hints":["The user's May Japan trip includes Tokyo and Osaka.","The user had already booked flights and accommodation for the Japan trip.","The Japan trip would be the user's 7th international trip that year.","The user averaged around 2 flights per month since November last year, mostly work-related.","The user had been to Singapore 3 times in the past 4 months, with the last trip on January 20th."],"confidence":0.95,"entity_refs":[{"entity_id":"entity:<0>","role":"primary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<4>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<5>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<6>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<7>","role":"mentioned"}],"evidence_kind":"direct","evidence_quote":"I'm actually planning a trip to Japan in May, which will be my 7th international trip this year. I've already booked my flights and accommodation, and I'm excited to explore Tokyo and Osaka.","evidence_reason":"The quote directly states the Japan trip timing, destinations, booking status, and its place in the user's annual international travel count.","importance":3,"keywords":["Japan","Tokyo","Osaka","May trip","7th international trip","flights","accommodation","Singapore","work travel"],"note_type":"context","rationale":"This contextualizes the Japan trip inside a reusable travel-frequency pattern and booking state; exact facts can become claims, but the combined framing is useful for future travel planning.","scope_json":{"basis":"summarized","kind":"entity","text":"User's Japan trip and surrounding international/work travel pattern as of 2023-03-03"},"summary":"The user's May Japan trip to Tokyo and Osaka was already booked for flights and accommodation and would be their 7th international trip of the year. This trip sits within a broader high-frequency travel pattern, including about 2 flights per month since the previous November and several work-related Singapore trips.","tags":["travel_context","international_travel","work_travel"],"title":"Japan May trip already booked despite high flight frequency","valid_from":"2023-03-03T14:37:00+02:00","valid_to":null}]}
